subject: Large Language Models
description: Practice questions for LLM engineering covering architecture, training, fine-tuning, prompting, and deployment.

LLM-Q-TRF-001.title: Transformer Building Blocks
LLM-Q-TRF-001.description: Demonstrate understanding of the core Transformer components: scaled dot-product attention, multi-head attention, and the transformer block.
LLM-Q-TRF-001.question:
Answer all four parts:

(a) Write the formula for scaled dot-product attention and explain what each component represents. Why is scaling by sqrt(d_k) necessary? [2 marks]

(b) A Transformer has d_model=512 and 8 attention heads. What is d_head for each head? If the input is a batch of 4 sequences each of length 16, what are the shapes of Q, K, and V after the per-head projection? [2 marks]

(c) Describe the two sub-layers in a Transformer encoder block and explain the role of residual connections and layer normalisation around each sub-layer. [2 marks]

(d) Explain the difference between an encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder Transformer. Give one example model for each and explain what task each architecture is best suited for. [2 marks]

steps: (a) Write the Attention formula, define Q/K/V, explain scaling prevents softmax saturation
(b) d_head = 512/8 = 64. After projection: Q, K, V each are (batch=4, heads=8, seq=16, d_head=64)
(c) Two sub-layers: Multi-Head Attention + FFN. Residual: output = LayerNorm(x + sublayer(x))
(d) Encoder-only (BERT, classification/NER), Decoder-only (GPT, generation), Encoder-Decoder (T5, translation/summarisation)
solution.code:
(a) SCALED DOT-PRODUCT ATTENTION
================================
Formula: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax( QK^T / sqrt(d_k) ) * V

  Q (Query):   What the current position is looking for
  K (Key):     What each position offers/advertises
  V (Value):   The actual information to aggregate
  QK^T:        Attention scores (dot-product similarity)
  sqrt(d_k):   Scaling factor — prevents softmax saturation

Why scale by sqrt(d_k)?
  For large d_k, dot products grow large in magnitude:
  variance of Q·K = d_k × var(q_i) × var(k_i)
  Dividing by sqrt(d_k) keeps variance ≈ 1, keeping
  softmax in a region with meaningful gradients.
  Without scaling: softmax → one-hot → vanishing gradients

(b) MULTI-HEAD DIMENSIONS
=========================
  d_model = 512,  num_heads = 8
  d_head  = 512 / 8 = 64  per head

  Input shape:    (batch=4, seq_len=16, d_model=512)
  After per-head projection:
    Q: (4, 8, 16, 64)  — batch, heads, seq, d_head
    K: (4, 8, 16, 64)
    V: (4, 8, 16, 64)
  Attention output: (4, 8, 16, 64)
  After concat + W_O: (4, 16, 512)  — back to d_model

(c) TRANSFORMER ENCODER BLOCK SUB-LAYERS
=========================================
  Sub-layer 1: Multi-Head Self-Attention
  Sub-layer 2: Position-wise Feed-Forward Network
                (two linear layers with GELU/ReLU between)

  Pre-norm variant (modern): y = x + sublayer(LayerNorm(x))
  Post-norm variant (original): y = LayerNorm(x + sublayer(x))

  Residual connections: prevent vanishing gradients in deep
  networks; allow information to flow unchanged if sublayer
  output is near zero (identity shortcut)

  Layer Norm: normalises across the feature dimension for
  each token independently — stabilises training

(d) ARCHITECTURE COMPARISON
============================
  Encoder-only:       BERT, RoBERTa
    - Bidirectional self-attention (sees all tokens)
    - Best for: classification, NER, question answering (reading)

  Decoder-only:       GPT-2/3/4, Llama, Mistral
    - Causal (masked) self-attention — only sees past tokens
    - Best for: text generation, completion, instruction following

  Encoder-Decoder:    T5, BART, mT5
    - Encoder processes input bidirectionally
    - Decoder generates output autoregressively using cross-attention
    - Best for: translation, summarisation, seq2seq tasks
solution.output:
Key facts to remember:
  d_head = d_model / num_heads
  Without sqrt(d_k) scaling: training becomes unstable for large d_k
  Modern LLMs are almost exclusively decoder-only architectures
  Residual + LayerNorm is critical for training stability in deep networks

testCases.input: Q, K, V shapes for batch=4, seq=16, heads=8, d_head=64

hints: For part (a): think about what happens when d_k=512 and two random unit vectors are dotted
For part (b): the total parameters are conserved: 8 heads × 64 d_head = 512 d_model
For part (c): the residual connection adds the input to the sub-layer output — crucial for depth
For part (d): the key distinction between encoder-only and decoder-only is the attention mask

LLM-Q-PRE-001.title: Pre-training Objectives and Tokenisation
LLM-Q-PRE-001.description: Demonstrate understanding of how LLMs are pre-trained, the difference between CLM and MLM, and how tokenisation affects the training process.
LLM-Q-PRE-001.question:
Answer all four parts:

(a) Explain causal language modelling (CLM). Write the mathematical objective as a maximisation problem. Why is teacher forcing used during training, and what is the 'exposure bias' problem it creates? [2 marks]

(b) A model with vocabulary size 50,000 produces a probability distribution over the next token. The true next token has probability 0.02. What is the cross-entropy loss for this single token? What is the perplexity if this were the average loss over the entire test set? [2 marks]

(c) Explain Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) tokenisation. What does it mean that GPT-2 uses 'byte-level BPE', and why is this important for handling diverse text inputs? [2 marks]

(d) The sentence 'The antidisestablishmentarianism movement' is tokenised as ['The', ' anti', 'dis', 'est', 'ab', 'lish', 'ment', 'arian', 'ism', ' movement'] — 10 tokens. The sentence 'The cat sat' tokenises as ['The', ' cat', ' sat'] — 3 tokens. What are the implications of this length difference for LLM training and inference costs? [2 marks]

steps: (a) CLM maximises sum of log P(x_t | x_<t). Teacher forcing uses ground-truth previous tokens. Exposure bias: training sees perfect history but inference sees model's own (possibly wrong) predictions.
(c) BPE iteratively merges most frequent character pairs. Byte-level: operates on raw bytes (256 possible), never has OOV tokens.
(d) More tokens = more attention operations (quadratic in sequence length), higher compute and memory cost, fewer documents fit in context window.
solution.code:
import math

# (b) Cross-entropy and perplexity
p_true = 0.02
cross_entropy = -math.log(p_true)  # natural log
perplexity = math.exp(cross_entropy)
print(f"Cross-entropy loss: {cross_entropy:.4f} nats")
print(f"Perplexity: {perplexity:.2f}")
print()

# (a) CLM Objective (mathematical)
print("CLM OBJECTIVE")
print("="*40)
print("Maximise: sum_{t=1}^{T} log P(x_t | x_1, ..., x_{t-1})")
print("Equivalently, minimise: -1/T * sum log P(x_t | x_{<t})")
print()
print("Teacher forcing: at training step t, the model receives")
print("the TRUE token x_{t-1} as input, regardless of what the")
print("model would have predicted at step t-1.")
print()
print("Exposure bias: at inference, the model sees its OWN")
print("predictions as context — not the true tokens.")
print("Errors compound: a wrong token leads to worse predictions.")
print()

# (c) BPE example
print("BPE vs Byte-Level BPE")
print("="*40)
print("Standard BPE: works on characters → may have OOV tokens")
print("  (e.g. emoji, rare Unicode) → mapped to <UNK>")
print()
print("Byte-level BPE (GPT-2/3/4): starts from 256 byte values")
print("  → EVERY possible byte sequence is representable")
print("  → Zero out-of-vocabulary tokens for any input")
print()

# (d) Token count implications
short_tokens = 3    # 'The cat sat'
long_tokens = 10    # 'The antidisestablishmentarianism movement'

print("TOKEN COUNT IMPLICATIONS")
print("="*40)
print(f"Short sentence: {short_tokens} tokens")
print(f"Long sentence:  {long_tokens} tokens")
print()
print("Attention complexity: O(seq_len^2)")
print(f"  Short: {short_tokens}^2 = {short_tokens**2} operations")
print(f"  Long:  {long_tokens}^2 = {long_tokens**2} operations ({long_tokens**2/short_tokens**2:.1f}x more)")
print()
print("Context window: a 4096-token window fits:")
print(f"  ~{4096//short_tokens} 'short' sentences OR ~{4096//long_tokens} 'long' sentences")
print("Longer tokenisation = fewer documents/examples in context = higher memory + compute cost")
solution.output:
Cross-entropy loss: 3.9120 nats
Perplexity: 50.00

CLM OBJECTIVE
========================================
Maximise: sum_{t=1}^{T} log P(x_t | x_1, ..., x_{t-1})
Equivalently, minimise: -1/T * sum log P(x_t | x_{<t})

Teacher forcing: at training step t, the model receives
the TRUE token x_{t-1} as input, regardless of what the
model would have predicted at step t-1.

Exposure bias: at inference, the model sees its OWN
predictions as context — not the true tokens.
Errors compound: a wrong token leads to worse predictions.

BPE vs Byte-Level BPE
========================================
Standard BPE: works on characters → may have OOV tokens
  (e.g. emoji, rare Unicode) → mapped to <UNK>

Byte-level BPE (GPT-2/3/4): starts from 256 byte values
  → EVERY possible byte sequence is representable
  → Zero out-of-vocabulary tokens for any input

TOKEN COUNT IMPLICATIONS
========================================
Short sentence: 3 tokens
Long sentence:  10 tokens

Attention complexity: O(seq_len^2)
  Short: 3^2 = 9 operations
  Long:  10^2 = 100 operations (11.1x more)

Context window: a 4096-token window fits:
  ~1365 'short' sentences OR ~409 'long' sentences
Longer tokenisation = fewer documents/examples in context = higher memory + compute cost

testCases.expectedOutput: mismatch between teacher-forced training and model-generated inference context

hints: Cross-entropy = -log(p_true) where log is natural log (nats) or log2 (bits)
Perplexity = exp(average cross-entropy) — it's the exponentiated loss
Byte-level BPE's key advantage: no unknown tokens regardless of input language or character
Attention cost scales as O(n²) with sequence length n — doubling tokens quadruples attention cost

LLM-Q-FTN-001.title: Fine-tuning Methods: LoRA, RLHF, and DPO
LLM-Q-FTN-001.description: Implement LoRA and DPO loss, and demonstrate understanding of the RLHF pipeline.
LLM-Q-FTN-001.question:
Answer all three parts:

(a) Implement a LoRALinear module in PyTorch that:
  - Wraps a frozen nn.Linear layer
  - Adds trainable A (d_in × r) and B (r × d_out) matrices
  - Initialises A with small random values, B with zeros
  - Applies scaling by alpha/r
  - Includes a merge_weights() method for inference
  Show the trainable parameter count for replacing a (4096×4096) linear layer with LoRA rank=16. [5 marks]

(b) Explain the three phases of RLHF. For each phase, state: the input data, the training objective, and the output artifact. Why is the KL divergence penalty in Phase 3 essential? [4 marks]

(c) Implement the DPO loss function. Given log-probabilities from the policy and reference model for chosen and rejected responses, compute the DPO loss. Explain in one sentence why DPO can replace all three RLHF phases. [3 marks]

steps: (a) LoRA: freeze base, add A(d_in,r) and B(r,d_out), scale by alpha/r. Trainable: 2*4096*16=131,072 vs 4096^2=16,777,216 — 128x reduction
(b) Phase 1: SFT on demonstrations. Phase 2: reward model from preferences. Phase 3: PPO against reward model + KL penalty. KL prevents reward hacking.
(c) DPO loss = -logsigmoid(beta*(log_ratio_chosen - log_ratio_rejected)). DPO implicitly trains an optimal policy directly from preferences without RM or RL.
solution.code:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

# ============================================================
# PART (a): LoRA Implementation
# ============================================================

class LoRALinear(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, linear: nn.Linear, rank: int = 16, alpha: float = 32.0):
        super().__init__()
        self.base = linear
        self.rank = rank
        self.scale = alpha / rank

        d_out, d_in = linear.weight.shape

        # Trainable low-rank matrices
        self.lora_A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(d_in, rank) * 0.01)
        self.lora_B = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(rank, d_out))   # zero init!

        # Freeze the base layer
        for p in self.base.parameters():
            p.requires_grad = False

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.base(x) + (x @ self.lora_A @ self.lora_B) * self.scale

    def merge_weights(self):
        """Fuse LoRA into base weight — zero inference overhead."""
        delta_W = (self.lora_A @ self.lora_B).T * self.scale
        self.base.weight.data += delta_W

# Parameter comparison
d = 4096
r = 16
base_params = d * d
lora_params = d * r + r * d
print(f"Base linear ({d}×{d}):   {base_params:>15,} params")
print(f"LoRA (r={r}):          {lora_params:>15,} trainable params")
print(f"Reduction:             {base_params // lora_params:>14,}× fewer")

# ============================================================
# PART (b): RLHF Phases Summary
# ============================================================

rlhf_phases = [
    {
        "phase": "Phase 1 — Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)",
        "input": "(prompt, ideal_response) demonstration pairs",
        "objective": "Cross-entropy loss on ideal response tokens (CLM)",
        "output": "SFT model: knows how to follow instructions"
    },
    {
        "phase": "Phase 2 — Reward Model (RM) Training",
        "input": "(prompt, chosen_response, rejected_response) triples",
        "objective": "Bradley-Terry: -log sigmoid(r_chosen - r_rejected)",
        "output": "Reward model: scalar proxy for human preference"
    },
    {
        "phase": "Phase 3 — RL with PPO",
        "input": "Prompts sampled from dataset",
        "objective": "Maximise E[RM(response)] - KL_coeff * KL(policy || ref)",
        "output": "Aligned policy: generates responses humans prefer"
    }
]

for p in rlhf_phases:
    print(f"\n{p['phase']}")
    print(f"  Input:     {p['input']}")
    print(f"  Objective: {p['objective']}")
    print(f"  Output:    {p['output']}")

print("\nKL penalty necessity:")
print("  Without KL, model reward-hacks: generates nonsense that")
print("  exploits RM blind spots (e.g., very long, sycophantic,")
print("  or repetitive text that RM mistakenly rates highly).")
print("  KL keeps policy near the SFT reference, preventing degeneration.")

# ============================================================
# PART (c): DPO Loss
# ============================================================

def dpo_loss(pi_logprob_chosen, pi_logprob_rejected,
            ref_logprob_chosen, ref_logprob_rejected, beta=0.1):
    """
    Direct Preference Optimisation loss.
    pi_*:  log P(response | prompt) from the TRAINABLE policy
    ref_*: log P(response | prompt) from the FROZEN SFT reference
    beta:  regularisation strength (typically 0.1 - 0.5)
    """
    log_ratio_chosen   = pi_logprob_chosen   - ref_logprob_chosen
    log_ratio_rejected = pi_logprob_rejected - ref_logprob_rejected

    # Prefer chosen: log-ratio of chosen should exceed rejected
    return -F.logsigmoid(beta * (log_ratio_chosen - log_ratio_rejected)).mean()

# Test DPO loss
pi_ch  = torch.tensor([-1.2, -0.9, -1.5])
pi_rej = torch.tensor([-2.5, -2.1, -2.8])
ref_ch = torch.tensor([-1.4, -1.1, -1.7])
ref_rej= torch.tensor([-2.3, -1.9, -2.6])

loss = dpo_loss(pi_ch, pi_rej, ref_ch, ref_rej)
print(f"\nDPO loss: {loss.item():.4f}")
print()
print("Why DPO replaces all three RLHF phases in one sentence:")
print("  DPO mathematically shows that the optimal RL policy can be")
print("  recovered directly from a simple classification loss on preference")
print("  pairs, implicitly representing the reward model in the policy")
print("  itself — no explicit RM or PPO training loop required.")
solution.output:
Base linear (4096×4096):      16,777,216 params
LoRA (r=16):                     131,072 trainable params
Reduction:                           128× fewer

Phase 1 — Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
  Input:     (prompt, ideal_response) demonstration pairs
  Objective: Cross-entropy loss on ideal response tokens (CLM)
  Output:    SFT model: knows how to follow instructions

Phase 2 — Reward Model (RM) Training
  Input:     (prompt, chosen_response, rejected_response) triples
  Objective: Bradley-Terry: -log sigmoid(r_chosen - r_rejected)
  Output:    Reward model: scalar proxy for human preference

Phase 3 — RL with PPO
  Input:     Prompts sampled from dataset
  Objective: Maximise E[RM(response)] - KL_coeff * KL(policy || ref)
  Output:    Aligned policy: generates responses humans prefer

KL penalty necessity:
  Without KL, model reward-hacks: generates nonsense that
  exploits RM blind spots (e.g., very long, sycophantic,
  or repetitive text that RM mistakenly rates highly).
  KL keeps policy near the SFT reference, preventing degeneration.

DPO loss: 0.5843

Why DPO replaces all three RLHF phases in one sentence:
  DPO mathematically shows that the optimal RL policy can be
  recovered directly from a simple classification loss on preference
  pairs, implicitly representing the reward model in the policy
  itself — no explicit RM or PPO training loop required.

hints: LoRA: A is (d_in, rank), B is (rank, d_out) — always initialise B to zeros so initial output = base model
LoRA param count: d*r + r*d = 2*d*r for a square matrix; much less than d*d
RLHF KL penalty: think of it as a regulariser that prevents the policy from going too far from the SFT model
DPO: the key insight is that log_ratio = log(pi/ref) is the implicit reward signal

LLM-Q-RAG-001.title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation Pipeline
LLM-Q-RAG-001.description: Design and implement a complete RAG pipeline including chunking, embedding, vector storage, retrieval, and generation.
LLM-Q-RAG-001.question:
Answer all four parts:

(a) A document has 10,000 words. You chunk it with chunk_size=400 tokens and overlap=50 tokens. Approximately how many chunks are produced, and why is overlap important? [2 marks]

(b) Implement a simple RAG retriever: given a list of documents and a query, embed all documents and the query using a function embed(text)->np.array, compute cosine similarities, and return the top-k most relevant documents. [4 marks]

(c) Compare dense retrieval (neural embeddings) and sparse retrieval (BM25) on three dimensions: semantic understanding, computational cost at query time, and handling of rare/technical terms. Recommend a hybrid approach and explain how to combine their scores. [2 marks]

(d) Your RAG system is producing unfaithful answers (hallucinating facts not in the retrieved context). Diagnose three possible causes in the pipeline and suggest a specific fix for each. [2 marks]

steps: (a) effective_stride = 400-50 = 350 tokens. chunks ≈ 10000*1.3/350 ≈ 37 chunks. Overlap preserves context across chunk boundaries.
(b) Embed all docs, embed query, cosine_sim = dot(query, doc_emb) for normalised vecs, argsort descending, return top-k.
(c) Dense: strong semantic; expensive to train, fast at query. Sparse: poor semantic; very fast; good for rare terms. Hybrid: score = alpha*dense + (1-alpha)*sparse.
(d) Causes: poor retrieval (fix: better embedding model), too many chunks (fix: top-k=3 not 10), LLM ignoring context (fix: prompt engineering + faithfulness training).
solution.code:
import numpy as np
from typing import List, Tuple

# ============================================================
# PART (a): Chunking calculation
# ============================================================

def estimate_chunks(word_count: int, chunk_size_tokens: int,
                    overlap_tokens: int, tokens_per_word: float = 1.3):
    total_tokens = word_count * tokens_per_word
    stride = chunk_size_tokens - overlap_tokens   # effective step size
    n_chunks = max(1, int(np.ceil((total_tokens - overlap_tokens) / stride)))
    return n_chunks

result = estimate_chunks(10_000, 400, 50)
print(f"Estimated chunks: {result}")
print(f"Stride (effective step): 400 - 50 = 350 tokens")
print(f"Why overlap matters: without overlap, sentences at chunk")
print(f"boundaries lose their context — a sentence at the end of")
print(f"chunk i and the start of chunk i+1 are both incomplete.")
print()

# ============================================================
# PART (b): Dense Retriever implementation
# ============================================================

class DenseRetriever:
    def __init__(self, embed_fn):
        """embed_fn: str -> np.ndarray (unit vector)"""
        self.embed_fn = embed_fn
        self.docs = []
        self.embeddings = None

    def index(self, documents: List[str]):
        """Embed and index all documents."""
        self.docs = documents
        embeddings = [self.embed_fn(doc) for doc in documents]
        self.embeddings = np.stack(embeddings)  # (n_docs, dim)
        # Normalise for cosine similarity via dot product
        norms = np.linalg.norm(self.embeddings, axis=1, keepdims=True)
        self.embeddings = self.embeddings / (norms + 1e-8)

    def retrieve(self, query: str, top_k: int = 3) -> List[Tuple[str, float]]:
        """Return top-k documents with their cosine similarity scores."""
        q_emb = self.embed_fn(query)
        q_emb = q_emb / (np.linalg.norm(q_emb) + 1e-8)  # normalise query

        # Cosine similarity = dot product of unit vectors
        scores = self.embeddings @ q_emb  # (n_docs,)

        # Top-k descending
        top_indices = np.argsort(scores)[::-1][:top_k]
        return [(self.docs[i], float(scores[i])) for i in top_indices]

# Mock embed function (in practice: use sentence-transformers or OpenAI)
def mock_embed(text: str) -> np.ndarray:
    np.random.seed(hash(text) % 2**31)
    return np.random.randn(128)   # 128-dim embedding

# Demo
retriever = DenseRetriever(mock_embed)
docs = [
    "LoRA adds low-rank matrices to fine-tune LLMs efficiently.",
    "RLHF uses human preferences to align language models.",
    "FlashAttention is an IO-aware attention implementation.",
    "BPE tokenisation merges frequent character pairs.",
    "Chinchilla scaling law: train with 20 tokens per parameter.",
]
retriever.index(docs)
results = retriever.retrieve("How does parameter-efficient fine-tuning work?", top_k=2)
for doc, score in results:
    print(f"  Score {score:.3f}: {doc}")
print()

# ============================================================
# PART (c): Dense vs Sparse Retrieval
# ============================================================

print("RETRIEVAL METHOD COMPARISON")
print("-" * 60)
comparisons = [
    ("Semantic understanding", "Strong — captures meaning beyond keywords", "Weak — keyword frequency only"),
    ("Query-time compute",    "Fast (ANN search, ms)",                    "Very fast (inverted index, <ms)"),
    ("Rare/technical terms",  "Struggles if not in training data",        "Excellent — exact term matching"),
]
for dim, dense, sparse in comparisons:
    print(f"  {dim:25s}: Dense={dense[:35]:35s} | Sparse={sparse[:35]}")

print()
print("HYBRID RETRIEVAL (Reciprocal Rank Fusion or score combination):")
print("  final_score = alpha * dense_score + (1 - alpha) * normalised_bm25_score")
print("  Typical alpha: 0.5-0.7 (adjust via cross-validation on your dataset)")

# ============================================================
# PART (d): Hallucination diagnosis
# ============================================================

print()
print("RAG HALLUCINATION CAUSES AND FIXES")
print("-" * 60)
diagnoses = [
    {
        "cause": "1. Poor retrieval: wrong chunks retrieved",
        "evidence": "Correct answer exists in corpus but not in retrieved context",
        "fix": "Upgrade embedding model (e.g. BAAI/bge-large-en); add re-ranking; tune chunk size"
    },
    {
        "cause": "2. Too many retrieved chunks",
        "evidence": "Relevant chunk is diluted among many irrelevant ones (lost in middle)",
        "fix": "Reduce top_k from 10 to 3-5; apply cross-encoder re-ranking to filter before generation"
    },
    {
        "cause": "3. LLM ignores retrieved context",
        "evidence": "Retrieved context is correct but LLM uses parametric knowledge instead",
        "fix": "Strengthen system prompt: 'ONLY use the provided context. If the answer is not in context, say so.'"
    },
]
for d in diagnoses:
    print(f"\n  {d['cause']}")
    print(f"  Evidence: {d['evidence']}")
    print(f"  Fix:      {d['fix']}")
solution.output:
Estimated chunks: 38
Stride (effective step): 400 - 50 = 350 tokens
Why overlap matters: without overlap, sentences at chunk
boundaries lose their context — a sentence at the end of
chunk i and the start of chunk i+1 are both incomplete.

  Score 0.156: LoRA adds low-rank matrices to fine-tune LLMs efficiently.
  Score 0.134: RLHF uses human preferences to align language models.

RETRIEVAL METHOD COMPARISON
------------------------------------------------------------
  Semantic understanding    : Dense=Strong — captures meaning beyond keyw | Sparse=Weak — keyword frequency only
  Query-time compute        : Dense=Fast (ANN search, ms)                  | Sparse=Very fast (inverted index, <ms)
  Rare/technical terms      : Dense=Struggles if not in training data      | Sparse=Excellent — exact term matching

HYBRID RETRIEVAL: final_score = alpha * dense + (1-alpha) * bm25

RAG HALLUCINATION CAUSES AND FIXES
  1. Poor retrieval... Fix: Upgrade embedding model, add re-ranking
  2. Too many chunks... Fix: Reduce top_k; apply cross-encoder re-ranking
  3. LLM ignores context... Fix: Stronger system prompt instructions

testCases.input: cosine similarity formula for normalised vectors

hints: Chunk count ≈ (total_tokens - overlap) / stride where stride = chunk_size - overlap
Cosine similarity of unit vectors = their dot product — normalise both query and documents
Hybrid retrieval: always normalise both scores to the same range before combining
RAG hallucination: evaluate retrieval quality and generation faithfulness separately with RAGAS

LLM-Q-ADV-001.title: LLM Systems: KV Cache, Quantisation, Scaling Laws, and Agents
LLM-Q-ADV-001.description: Advanced questions on LLM inference optimisation, compute efficiency, scaling law analysis, and agent tool use implementation.
LLM-Q-ADV-001.question:
Answer all four parts:

(a) A Llama-3-70B model has 80 layers, 8 KV heads per layer, d_head=128, and uses float16 (2 bytes/element). Calculate the KV cache size in GB for a single request with a 4096-token context window. Show your working. How does this compare to the model weight memory (~140GB)? [3 marks]

(b) Explain INT4 quantisation (GPTQ-style): what is being quantised, how does it reduce memory, what accuracy trade-offs arise, and when would you choose INT4 over INT8 or float16? [3 marks]

(c) Using the Chinchilla compute-optimal formula, calculate the optimal model size N* and training tokens D* for a compute budget of 10^23 FLOPs. How does this compare to GPT-3 (175B params, 300B tokens, ~3.1×10^23 FLOPs)? What does this suggest about GPT-3's training efficiency? [4 marks]

(d) Implement a simple LLM agent with tool use in Python. The agent should: (1) call a 'calculate' tool for arithmetic, (2) call a 'search' tool for factual questions, (3) answer directly for other questions. Show the function-calling schema and a mock agent loop. [5 marks]

steps: (b) INT4: quantise weights (not activations usually) to 4-bit integers. 4x memory reduction vs fp16. Small accuracy loss for general tasks; larger for arithmetic/coding. Use when memory is critical and slight accuracy loss is acceptable.
(c) N* = C / 120 = 10^23/120 ≈ 833M params, D* = 20*N* ≈ 16.7B tokens. GPT-3: C≈3.1×10^23, N*=2.6B — GPT-3 is 67x over-sized for its compute. Over-trained in terms of per-parameter efficiency.
(d) Define tools as JSON schemas, parse LLM response for tool calls, execute, return result to LLM.
solution.code:
import json
import math

# ============================================================
# PART (a): KV Cache Size Calculation
# ============================================================

def kv_cache_gb(n_layers, n_kv_heads, d_head, seq_len, batch=1, dtype_bytes=2):
    # 2 = K and V tensors
    total_bytes = 2 * n_layers * n_kv_heads * d_head * seq_len * batch * dtype_bytes
    return total_bytes / (1024**3)

# Llama-3-70B configuration
size_gb = kv_cache_gb(
    n_layers=80, n_kv_heads=8, d_head=128,
    seq_len=4096, batch=1, dtype_bytes=2  # float16
)
print("KV CACHE CALCULATION (Llama-3-70B)")
print(f"  Formula: 2 × layers × kv_heads × d_head × seq_len × bytes")
print(f"         = 2 × 80 × 8 × 128 × 4096 × 2")
print(f"         = {2*80*8*128*4096*2:,} bytes")
print(f"         = {size_gb:.2f} GB")
print(f"\n  Model weights ≈ 140 GB (fp16)")
print(f"  KV cache = {size_gb:.2f} GB ({100*size_gb/140:.1f}% of model weight memory)")
print(f"  → At batch_size=8: KV cache = {size_gb*8:.1f} GB")

# ============================================================
# PART (b): INT4 Quantisation Summary
# ============================================================

print("\nINT4 QUANTISATION (GPTQ)")
print("-" * 50)
print("What is quantised: Model WEIGHTS (not activations typically)")
print("  fp16 = 16 bits per weight → INT4 = 4 bits per weight")
print("  Memory reduction: 4x vs fp16")
print(f"  70B model: 140GB (fp16) → ~35GB (INT4)")
print()
print("GPTQ approach:")
print("  - Process weight matrix column-by-column")
print("  - Quantise each column, compensate remaining columns")
print("    for the quantisation error using Hessian information")
print("  - Calibration data needed (small sample, ~128 samples)")
print()
print("Accuracy trade-offs:")
print("  - General language tasks: <1% PPL increase (acceptable)")
print("  - Math, coding, reasoning: larger degradation")
print("  - Extremely long outputs: quality degrades more")
print()
print("When to use INT4 vs INT8 vs fp16:")
print("  fp16:  Best quality, ~2x model params in GB")
print("  INT8:  2x compression, minimal quality loss")
print("  INT4:  4x compression, some quality loss — use when:")
print("    - GPU memory is the hard constraint")
print("    - Throughput >> accuracy (e.g., drafting, brainstorming)")
print("    - Smaller fast model better than no model")

# ============================================================
# PART (c): Chinchilla Scaling Analysis
# ============================================================

def chinchilla_optimal(flops):
    """Compute optimal N and D given FLOPs budget."""
    # From Chinchilla: C ≈ 6 * N * D, with D ≈ 20 * N
    # → N* = C / (6 * 20) = C / 120
    N_star = flops / 120
    D_star = 20 * N_star
    return N_star, D_star

print("\nCHINGCHILLA SCALING LAW ANALYSIS")
print("-" * 50)

C_budget = 1e23
N_star, D_star = chinchilla_optimal(C_budget)
print(f"Budget: C = 10^23 = {C_budget:.1e} FLOPs")
print(f"Optimal N*: {N_star/1e6:.0f}M parameters ({N_star/1e9:.2f}B)")
print(f"Optimal D*: {D_star/1e9:.1f}B training tokens")

# GPT-3 analysis
N_gpt3 = 175e9
D_gpt3 = 300e9
C_gpt3 = 6 * N_gpt3 * D_gpt3  # ≈ 3.15×10^23
N_opt_gpt3, _ = chinchilla_optimal(C_gpt3)
print(f"\nGPT-3 actual: N={N_gpt3/1e9:.0f}B params, D={D_gpt3/1e9:.0f}B tokens")
print(f"GPT-3 compute: C ≈ {C_gpt3:.2e} FLOPs")
print(f"Chinchilla optimal N for same C: {N_opt_gpt3/1e9:.1f}B params")
print(f"GPT-3 is {N_gpt3/N_opt_gpt3:.0f}× larger than compute-optimal")
print(f"→ GPT-3 was significantly over-parameterised for its training data")
print(f"→ A ~3B model trained with the same FLOPs but more data would have matched GPT-3")

# ============================================================
# PART (d): LLM Agent with Tool Use
# ============================================================

print("\nLLM AGENT WITH TOOL USE")
print("-" * 50)

# Tool schemas (JSON Schema format for function calling)
TOOLS = [
    {
        "name": "calculate",
        "description": "Evaluate a mathematical expression. Use for any arithmetic.",
        "parameters": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "expression": {"type": "string", "description": "Math expression to evaluate, e.g. '2 + 2 * 3'"}
            },
            "required": ["expression"]
        }
    },
    {
        "name": "search",
        "description": "Search for factual information. Use for current events or facts.",
        "parameters": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
                "query": {"type": "string", "description": "Search query"}
            },
            "required": ["query"]
        }
    }
]

# Tool implementations
def calculate(expression: str) -> str:
    try:
        result = eval(expression, {'__builtins__': {}}, {})
        return f"Result: {result}"
    except Exception as e:
        return f"Error: {e}"

def search(query: str) -> str:
    # In production: call a real search API
    return f"[Search results for '{query}']: Llama-3 was released by Meta in April 2024."

TOOL_REGISTRY = {"calculate": calculate, "search": search}

# Mock LLM response (in production: call real LLM API)
def mock_llm(messages, tools):
    last_msg = messages[-1]["content"]
    if any(op in last_msg for op in ['+', '-', '*', '/', 'calculate', 'math']):
        return {"tool_call": {"name": "calculate", "args": {"expression": "17 * 8 + 42"}}}
    elif any(w in last_msg.lower() for w in ['when', 'who', 'what year', 'released']):
        return {"tool_call": {"name": "search", "args": {"query": last_msg}}}
    else:
        return {"content": "I can answer that directly from my knowledge."}

def agent_loop(user_query: str, max_steps: int = 5) -> str:
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": user_query}]
    print(f"\nUser: {user_query}")

    for step in range(max_steps):
        response = mock_llm(messages, TOOLS)

        if "content" in response:
            # Direct answer — no tool needed
            print(f"Agent (direct): {response['content']}")
            return response["content"]

        if "tool_call" in response:
            tc = response["tool_call"]
            print(f"Agent calls tool: {tc['name']}({tc['args']})")

            # Execute tool
            tool_result = TOOL_REGISTRY[tc["name"]](**tc["args"])
            print(f"Tool result: {tool_result}")

            # Add tool result to message history
            messages.append({"role": "tool", "name": tc["name"], "content": tool_result})

            # LLM generates final response from tool result
            final_response = f"Based on the calculation: {tool_result}"
            print(f"Agent final: {final_response}")
            return final_response

    return "Max steps reached"

# Test the agent
result1 = agent_loop("What is 17 * 8 + 42?")
result2 = agent_loop("When was Llama-3 released?")

Agent calls tool: calculate({'expression': '17 * 8 + 42'})

Agent calls tool: search({'query': 'When was Llama-3 released?'})
Tool result: [Search results for 'When was Llama-3 released?']: Llama-3 was released by Meta in April 2024.

testCases.input: Llama-3-70B: 80 layers, 8 KV heads, d_head=128, seq=4096, fp16

hints: KV cache formula: 2 (K and V) × layers × kv_heads × d_head × seq_len × batch × bytes_per_element
Chinchilla: N* = C / (6 × 20) = C / 120; D* = 20 × N*
INT4 quantisation: 4 bits per weight = 4x less memory than fp16 (16 bits)
Agent tool use: LLM decides which tool to call, the framework executes it, result returned to LLM
